title: research
text:
I am interested in developing statistical methodologies for analysis of complex datasets. Most of my research is focused broadly in the fields of statistical learning, data mining, predictive modeling, and the application of Bayesian methodologies. My PhD dissertation can be found here.
I also like to think about ways to cater to a diverse population of students, reflect on how to improve as a teacher, and develop engaging pedagogical tools. Therein lies my interest in statistics education. Lately, I have also been interested in the history of statistics.

publications

The Greenhouse Effect: Using Student-Generated Agricultural Data to Warm Up Students for Data-Based Decision Making (link)
Some Bayesian Biclustering Methods: Modeling and Inference (link)
Modeling and Inference for Mixtures of Simple Symmetric Exponential Families of \(p\)-Dimensional Distributions for Vectors with Binary Coordinates (link)

title: Welcome!
text:
Hi there! I’m Abhishek (pronounced “uh-bhe(like he)-shake”). I am an Associate Professor of Statistics in the Department of Mathematics, Statistics, and Computer Science at Lawrence University, Appleton, Wisconsin. I am originally from Kolkata, India. I completed my Bachelors in Statistics from St. Xavier’s College, Kolkata, and my Masters from IIT, Kanpur. I earned my Ph.D. in Statistics from Iowa State University.
I enjoy teaching and relish being in the company of students. Please visit my teaching and research pages to learn more about my academic activities.
Outside the academic world, I enjoy running, biking, and going for hikes. My other interests include watching soccer as well as fiddling with my guitar.

title: teaching
text:
For more information about mathematics and statistics-data science courses offered at LU, please visit here. Information about computer science courses can be found here. The major/minor requirements of our mathematics and related programs can be found here.
At Lawrence, I have been the instructor for a variety of statistics and data science courses, and our flagship First-Year Studies course.

STAT 107: Principles of Statistics
CMSC/STAT 205: Data‑Scientific Programming
CMSC/LING/STAT 208: Statistical Machine Learning
STAT 255: Statistical Modeling
MATH/STAT 340: Probability
MATH/STAT 445: Mathematical Statistics
STAT 450: Bayesian Statistics
STAT 698: Statistics Senior Seminar (co-instructor)
FRST 101: First‑Year Studies

I have also been the instructor and TA for the following courses at ISU.

STAT 104: Introduction to Statistics
STAT 226: Introduction to Business Statistics I
STAT 305: Engineering Statistics
STAT 330: Probability and Statistics for Computer Science
STAT 326: Introduction to Business Statistics II

title: R package
text: NOTE: This is a toy package created for expository purposes.

title: R package
text:
Installation
You can install the development version of toypackage from GitHub with:

# install.packages("remotes")
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("abhicc/toypackage")

title: R package
text:
Examples
This is a basic example which shows you how to solve a common problem:

library(toypackage)


strsplit1 example

first_subject_words <- toy_data$recalled_words[1]
strsplit1(first_subject_words, split = ", ")
#> [1] "cat"   "dog"   "mouse"



min_max_scale example

min_max_scale(toy_data$test_score)
#> [1] 0.0000000 0.8043478 0.2173913 1.0000000 0.4782609



theme_academic example

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(toy_data, aes(x = reaction_time, y = test_score)) +
geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
labs(title = "Faster Reaction Times Correlate with Higher Scores",
x = "Reaction Time (seconds)", y = "Scaled Test Score (0 to 1)") +
theme_academic(base_size = 14)

title: text analysis project
text:
NOTE: This demonstration has been adapted from the MDSR (3e) textbook.

resources

arXiv.org



packages

library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(tidytext)
library(textdata)
library(mdsr)
library(stopwords)
library(slam)
library(tm)



data
We can import data using the aRxiv package. We won’t run it here since it strains the arXiv server.

library(aRxiv)

df <- arxiv_search(
  query = 'ti:"Data Science"',
  limit = 20000,
  batchsize = 100
)

# glimpse(df)

Instead, for this case study we will use some already-collected data from the mdsr package.

library(mdsr) 

data(DataSciencePapers)

?DataSciencePapers

glimpse(DataSciencePapers)

initial data exploration and cleaning

  mutate(
    submitted = lubridate::ymd_hms(submitted), 
    updated = lubridate::ymd_hms(updated)

corpora and tokenization

  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = abstract, token = "words") |>
  count(id, word, sort = TRUE)

arxiv_words <- DataSciencePapers |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = abstract, token = "words") |>
  anti_join(tidytext::get_stopwords(), by = "word")

arxiv_words |>
  count(id, word, sort = TRUE)

# A tibble: 93,681 × 3
   id           word        n
   <chr>        <chr>   <int>
 1 2007.03606v1 data       20
 2 1708.04664v1 data       19
 3 1606.06769v1 traffic    17
 4 1705.03451v2 data       17

 7 2003.10534v1 data       16
 8 1611.09874v1 ii         15
 9 1808.04849v1 data       15
10 1906.03418v1 data       15
# ℹ 93,671 more rows



arxiv_abstracts <- arxiv_words |>
  group_by(id) |>
  summarize(abstract_clean = paste(word, collapse = " "))

arxiv_papers <- DataSciencePapers |>
  left_join(arxiv_abstracts, by = "id")



wordcloud

library(wordcloud)

set.seed(4302026)
arxiv_papers |>
  pull(abstract_clean) |>
  wordcloud(
    max.words = 40, 
    scale = c(8, 1), 
    colors = topo.colors(n = 30), 
    random.color = TRUE
  )










sentiment analysis
We will use the AFINN lexicon, where each word is associated with an integer value ranging from -5 to 5, for our sentiment analysis purposes. When prompted, make sure to download the lexicon.

afinn <- tidytext::get_sentiments("afinn")

head(afinn)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  word       value
  <chr>      <dbl>
1 abandon       -2
2 abandoned     -2
3 abandons      -2
4 abducted      -2
5 abduction     -2
6 abductions    -2


We can join this lexicon with our data to calculate a sentiment score.

arxiv_words |> 
  inner_join(afinn, by = "word") |>
  select(word, id, value)

# A tibble: 7,397 × 3
   word      id                 value
   <chr>     <chr>              <dbl>
 1 ambitious astro-ph/0701361v1     2
 2 powerful  astro-ph/0701361v1     2
 3 impotent  astro-ph/0701361v1    -2
 4 like      astro-ph/0701361v1     2
 5 agree     astro-ph/0701361v1     1
 6 better    0901.2805v1            2
 7 better    0901.2805v1            2
 8 better    0901.2805v1            2
 9 improve   0901.2805v1            2
10 support   0901.3118v2            2
# ℹ 7,387 more rows



arxiv_sentiments <- arxiv_words |> 
  left_join(afinn, by = "word") |>
  group_by(id) |>
  summarize(num_words = n(),
    sentiment = sum(value, na.rm = TRUE), 
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(sentiment_per_word = sentiment / num_words) |>
  arrange(desc(sentiment))

arxiv_papers <- arxiv_papers |>
  left_join(arxiv_sentiments, by = "id")

arxiv_papers |>
  skim(sentiment, sentiment_per_word)

Variable type: numeric

The average sentiment score of these papers is 4, but they range from -26 to 39. Since abstracts with more words might accrue a higher sentiment score, we control for abstract length by dividing by the number of words. The paper with the highest sentiment score per word had a score of 0.333. Let’s take a closer look at the most positive abstract.

most_positive <- arxiv_papers |>
  filter(sentiment_per_word == max(sentiment_per_word)) |>
  pull(abstract)

strwrap(most_positive)

[1] "Data science is creating very exciting trends as well as significant"  
[2] "controversy. A critical matter for the healthy development of data"    
[3] "science in its early stages is to deeply understand the nature of data"
[4] "and data science, and to discuss the various pitfalls. These important"
[5] "issues motivate the discussions in this article."                      


Let’s explore if there are time trends or differences between different disciplines.

ggplot(data = arxiv_papers, 
  aes(x = submitted, y = sentiment_per_word, 
    color = field == "cs")) + 
  geom_smooth(se = TRUE) + 
  scale_color_brewer("Computer Science?", palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = "Date submitted", y = "Sentiment score per word")










bigrams

arxiv_bigrams <- arxiv_papers |>
  unnest_tokens(
    output = arxiv_bigram, 
    input = abstract_clean, 
    token = "ngrams", 
    n = 2
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(arxiv_bigram, id)
